name: Bulk Synchronous Parallel (BSP)
def: A parallel model structuring computation as a sequence of supersteps, each one: local compute, then communication, then a global barrier.
problem: Reason about and cost parallel/distributed algorithms with a clean separation of computation from communication, avoiding ad-hoc synchronisation.
mechanism: Each processor computes on local data, exchanges messages, and synchronises at a barrier that ends the superstep; messages sent in one superstep are guaranteed delivered before the next. Cost models as w + g·h + l (compute + communication + barrier latency).
benefits: Simple, deadlock-free structure — no fine-grained locking
Maps cleanly to iterative graph processing (Pregel-style)
limits: The barrier gates every superstep on the slowest worker (stragglers)
Poor fit for irregular or asynchronous, low-latency work
failures: Load imbalance stalling every superstep on one straggler
Too many supersteps for fine-grained iterative algorithms
whenNot: Asynchronous algorithms that tolerate stale state (use gossip / async)
myths: "BSP is just MapReduce" — BSP is iterative with stateful supersteps; MapReduce is a single map/shuffle/reduce pass

complexity.Note: Per-superstep ≈ w + g·h + l; total = Σ over supersteps
